title: Chapter 25: The Signal
content: Nine left turns. Four right. Seven speed bumps.

Lucy counted them in the dark. The hood smelled like mildew and something chemical — dry cleaning solvent, maybe, or industrial degreaser. It pressed against her nose and mouth with each breath, filling her lungs with the taste of someone else's panic. Her hands were zip-tied behind her back, the plastic biting into her wrists with every jolt of the van's suspension. Her ankles were bound too, though less tightly — they'd learned from Sublevel B that she could still kick.

The engine was diesel. She could tell by the intermittent clatter, the way it lagged half a second when the driver accelerated out of a turn. Not a smooth modern engine. An older van, probably a cargo model — no rear seats, which meant she was lying on a metal floor. She could feel every ridge and rivet through her jacket.

Nine left. Four right. Seven speed bumps. Approximately forty-three minutes of drive time at an average speed she estimated between thirty-five and forty-five miles per hour. That put her somewhere between twenty-five and thirty-two miles from wherever she'd been loaded.

She repeated the numbers. Locked them in. This was what people didn't understand about influencers. They thought content creators were performers — pretty faces reading scripts, selling products they'd never use. But performance was the smallest part of the job. The real work was observation. Cataloguing. You walked into a room and you read it — the light, the angles, the way sound bounced off the walls, the micro-expressions of everyone present. You filed it all because any of it might become content. Any of it might matter.
Lucy Chen had made her living by noticing what other people missed. Now it was keeping her alive.

The van braked hard. She slid forward until her shoulder struck something metal — a wheel well, probably. Then the engine cut. Doors opened. Footsteps on concrete — two sets, maybe three. Hands gripped her under the arms and hauled her out. Cold air hit the hood, and she felt the temperature difference immediately: warmer than Sublevel B's refrigerated corridors. Damp. Heavy.

They carried her — literally carried, like luggage — for what felt like two minutes. A door scraped open. Stone. She could hear it in the way sound changed — the echo dying against masonry instead of bouncing off tile. They went down three steps, turned right, walked another thirty seconds. Then she was lowered into a chair.

Different from Bay 7. The chair had armrests. Padded. Her zip-ties were cut and replaced with softer restraints — cloth, not plastic. Still binding, but not cutting. Someone was being careful with her now. That was either better or much, much worse.
A door closed. Locked. She was alone.

The hood came off on her terms. She worked it up with her chin, twisting her neck until the fabric slid over her forehead, then her eyes. Bright light flooded in — not the surgical white of Sublevel B, but a warmer tone. Older. Like sunlight filtered through dirty glass.
She was in a basement.

Not Sublevel B's clinical maze of white walls and seamless epoxy floors. This was something far older. Stone walls, rough-hewn and gray, rose maybe twelve feet to a ceiling crossed with dark wooden beams. The mortar between the stones was crumbling in places, and a thin line of moisture traced down one wall like a vein. But bolted to that medieval masonry was equipment that didn't belong: IV stands with digital monitors, a refrigerated blood storage unit humming quietly in the corner, bundles of medical-grade tubing coiled on stainless steel trays.
Old bones. New teeth.
Site C.

Lucy scanned the room. Six bays — not the endless rows of Sublevel B, but six individual alcoves carved into the stone, each separated by a partition that was half stone, half frosted glass. She was in Bay 2. A small metal plate on the wall confirmed it: BAY 2 — SITE C.
The other five bays were occupied.
She could see shapes through the frosted glass — reclined figures, the glow of monitoring equipment. And from Bay 4, a face turned toward her. A face she recognized.
Elena.

The woman from Bay 7 — the one who'd been counting her heartbeats, who'd whispered to Lucy through the walls of Sublevel B like a ghost passing messages between cells. Elena looked different here. Thinner. Her dark hair was lank and her skin had the waxy pallor of someone who hadn't seen real light in weeks. But her eyes were wide open. Alert. Alive.
She saw Lucy and her lips moved. No sound — the partitions were soundproofed, or close to it. But Lucy read the words the way she'd read a hundred camera setups, a thousand thumbnails:
*Still counting.*

Lucy almost smiled. Almost. The muscle memory of it was there — the reflex to perform reassurance, to broadcast strength. But this wasn't content. This was survival, and smiling at survival was how you stopped taking it seriously.
She nodded instead. A small, deliberate nod. *Me too.*
Bay 2 had a chair, a monitoring pedestal, an IV line she hadn't been hooked to yet, and an intercom.

Not a digital intercom. Not the sleek touchscreen panel from Sublevel B with its diagnostic mode and its tap-tap-tap vulnerability. This was analog — a square metal box mounted on the stone wall with a single button and a grated speaker. The kind of intercom that belonged in a building old enough to have actual architecture. The kind that connected to a building-wide PA system.
Lucy stared at it for ten minutes. Not because she didn't understand it, but because she understood it immediately, and she needed to be sure.

Her father had been a ham radio operator. KJ6-PTM — his call sign, still memorized, still as familiar as her own name. The apartment where she'd grown up had been criss-crossed with antenna wire, and the sound of shortwave static had been her lullaby. She'd sat on his lap at four years old, twisting the dial while he explained skip zones and propagation and how radio waves could bounce off the ionosphere and land a thousand miles away like a stone skipping water.

She'd hated it as a teenager. Too nerdy, too weird, too uncool for a girl who wanted to be seen. She'd left all of it behind — the radio, the static, the late-night conversations with strangers in distant time zones — and built herself into something that transmitted on a completely different frequency.
But she'd never forgotten.

The PA system was connected to an antenna on the roof. She could see the coaxial cable running up through a conduit in the stone ceiling. And old PA systems like this one — especially the ones installed in buildings that predated digital infrastructure — used simple amplification circuits. Circuits that, with the right manipulation, could push signal through the antenna instead of just through the speakers.
Shortwave. She could transmit on shortwave.

The thought landed in her chest like a second heartbeat. She was still bound to a chair in a vampire detention facility. She was still drained on a schedule she didn't control. But she had a voice, and she had an antenna, and somewhere out there, someone might be listening on a frequency that overlapped.
She just had to find the right moment to speak.
Half a continent away, Maya Reyes sat on the edge of a motel bed and stared at her phone.

Marcus's text was still on the screen. She'd read it eleven times. The kill switch. The podium. The three-second window. Every word was a key turning in a lock she hadn't known existed, and every word could also be a trap.
She held the phone up to her laptop camera so Chloe could see.

Chloe's face was tight on the screen — the compressed-pixel version of someone who'd been running on caffeine and adrenaline for two weeks straight. Her apartment in Seoul was dark behind her, lit only by the glow of four monitors running simultaneous data feeds.
"Can we trust him?" Chloe asked.
Maya shook her head slowly. "He warned me about Sublevel B. He was right about that."
"Or he was controlling the timeline. Telling you just enough to keep you moving in the direction Vlad wanted."
"Or he's a prisoner too." Maya's voice came out harder than she intended. She softened it. "Five hundred years of servitude, Chloe. He's not Vlad's partner — he's his slave. There's a difference."

Chloe was quiet for a moment. The debate played across her face in real time — the tactical brain processing variables, the human brain processing something else. "Either way," she said finally, "the information about the kill switch is either true or a trap. There's no third option."

"If it's true, we need it." Maya set the phone down on the bedspread. "If it's a trap, we'll know when we're at the podium. We'll have eyes on the location. If it's a setup, they'll have eyes on us too — and we'll see them before they see us, because we'll be looking."
"That's a lot of confidence for a plan that hinges on one former-familiar vampire's text message and a hundred-and-twenty-second firewall window."
"It's all we have."

Chloe exhaled. The sound crackled through the laptop speakers — a sharp, staticky burst that made Maya's fingers twitch. "Okay. Run it. But I'm monitoring the Aeterna frequency bands around the clock from now on. If there's any anomalous signal activity — anything — I'll catch it."
"Do that."

The call ended. Maya sat in the dark and listened to the motel room settle around her — the hum of the heating unit, the distant murmur of highway traffic, the thin whistle of wind through a window that didn't quite seal. Her phone was dark, Marcus's text extinguished. But the kill switch was still there, burning behind her eyes.
The podium. Three seconds. November 15th.
Fifteen days.
Lucy waited until the shift change.

She'd learned the rhythm of Site C faster than she'd learned Sublevel B — fewer guards, less automation, more human error. The facility was smaller, older, and its systems showed it. The monitoring equipment checked vitals every fifteen minutes. The guard walked the corridor every forty. The shift change happened at what she estimated was 2:00 AM — the hallway lights dimmed for exactly ninety seconds while the outgoing guard signed off and the incoming guard reviewed the log.
Ninety seconds. That was her window.

She'd been working on the intercom for three days. Not physically — there was nothing to disassemble, no panel to pry open. She'd been working on it the way she worked on everything: by observing. The button was a momentary switch — press to talk, release to listen. But the amplifier stayed hot for three seconds after the button was released, which meant there was a capacitor in the circuit. A capacitor that could, if she pressed the button in a rhythmic pattern at exactly the right frequency, overload the system's normal suppression band and push a signal through the rooftop antenna instead of just through the room speakers.
It was, technically, a hack. An analog hack. The kind her father would have called "creative signal abuse" with that satisfied grin he got when he made something do what it wasn't designed to do.
She pressed the button. One short. One long. One short. One long.
Morse code. K. M.
Just testing. Just seeing if the antenna would carry it.
The intercom crackled — a high, thin whine that climbed from below hearing into a register that made her teeth ache. Then it faded. The capacitor was hot. The antenna was live.
Lucy closed her eyes. Thought of her father. Thought of late nights at the kitchen table, the dial glowing amber, the static opening like a door.
She began to transmit.
*Lucy. Site C. Stone basement. Six women. Antenna on roof. I can transmit. Waiting for your signal.*

Each letter was a tap of the button. Each word was a journey up through the cable, through the conduit, through the antenna on the roof, out into the night sky. The signal bounced off nothing and everything. It rode the ionosphere the way her father had taught her, skipping across the dark like a stone across black water.
It was a whisper in a hurricane. It was a match in a cave.
It was enough.
Chloe's monitoring system flagged it at 4:47 AM Seoul time.

She was half-asleep at her desk, her coffee cold, the four monitors casting blue light across her face like deep water, when the alert pulsed. A frequency anomaly on the 14.230 MHz band — amateur radio territory, shortwave. The burst lasted less than two seconds, but Chloe's software was designed to capture and hold anything that didn't belong.
She played it back. Static. Then a pattern in the static — not digital noise, not environmental interference. Intentional. Rhythmic.
Morse code.

Her hands moved before her brain caught up. She pulled up the decoder and fed the recording through. The software parsed the dots and dashes with mechanical indifference, converting them into text that appeared on the screen one letter at a time:
L-U-C-Y
Chloe stopped breathing.
S-I-T-E-C
S-T-O-N-E-B-A-S-E-M-E-N-T
S-I-X-W-O-M-E-N
A-N-T-E-N-N-A-O-N-R-O-O-F
I-C-A-N-T-R-A-N-S-M-I-T
W-A-I-T-I-N-G-F-O-R-Y-O-U-R-S-I-G-N-A-L
She was reaching for her phone before the last word finished rendering.
Maya answered on the second ring. "What time is —"

"Listen to this." Chloe played the recording through the phone. The static hissed and popped, and then the pattern emerged — faint, distorted by distance and atmosphere, but unmistakably intentional. Unmistakably human.
Maya was quiet for a long time after it ended.
"Play it again," she said.
Chloe played it again.
"Lucy." Maya said the name like she was testing its weight. Like she was making sure it was real. "She's alive. She's in Site C. She's transmitting on shortwave."
"She knows we're out here. She's waiting for a signal."
Maya's hand tightened around the phone. The plastic creaked — a small sound, swallowed by the motel room's silence. "I need to send something back."
"Maya —"
"I need to send something back, Chloe."

A pause. Then Chloe said, "Okay. I can push a narrow-band pulse through the transmitter network. It'll overlap with shortwave frequencies — if her antenna's live, if her intercom's in receive mode, she might pick it up. Might."
"That's enough."
"Record your message. I'll compress it and format it for the system. But Maya — there's no confirmation. We won't know if she receives it. We just have to trust that the signal lands."

Maya was already opening her phone's voice recorder. Not her podcast app — this wasn't content. This wasn't for an audience. This was for one person. One woman in a stone basement with an analog intercom and a dead ham operator's knowledge, reaching up through the ionosphere with nothing but Morse code and stubbornness.
She pressed record.

"Lucy." Her voice was steady. It had to be steady. "I hear you. We're coming on IPO day. November 15th. At 9:30 AM Eastern, listen for the broadcast. When you hear my voice, get to the antenna. We're going to need you to transmit — everything you've seen, everything you know. You're not just surviving, Lucy. You're our final witness."
She paused. Steadied herself.
"Fifteen days."
She stopped recording. Sent the file to Chloe. And then she sat in the dark and waited.
On the other side of the country, in a stone basement that smelled of ozone and old wood, Lucy Chen sat in a padded chair in Bay 2 and listened to static.

The intercom was still hot. The capacitor still cycling. She'd transmitted everything she had, and now there was nothing to do but listen — the same way she'd listened as a child, her father's hand on her shoulder, the dial turning slowly through the noise.
The static shifted. Just barely. A whisper inside the whisper. A pulse inside the pulse.
She couldn't make out words. But the pattern was there — artificial, intentional, reaching down through the atmosphere from somewhere far away. Someone had heard her. Someone was answering.

Lucy closed her eyes. Her father's voice surfaced from memory, calm and certain: *The thing about radio, Little Lu, is that it doesn't care about walls. It doesn't care about distance. It only cares that someone's listening.*
She was listening.
And on the other end of the signal — separated by walls and monsters and fifteen days — so was Maya.
The connection was made. A thread of frequency stitching across the dark. Two women who had never stopped reaching, finally touching fingertips across the void.
The war was about to begin.
